title: What to do when a patient accuses you of causing a pre-existing problem
filename: What to do when a patient accuses you of causing a pre-existing problem.en.vtt
excerpt: So how do you tell a patient who attends at a follow-up saying that the treatment that you've given them has caused a specific problem that it was there all along [Music] well um this will happen a lot and it happens because if you think about what happens on the patient journey …

segments.text: so how do you tell a patient who attends at a follow-up saying that the treatment that you've given them has caused a specific problem that it was there all along [Music] well um this will happen a lot and it happens because if you think about what happens on the patient journey they will have a treatment often if they're on the anxious end of the spectrum or even if they're not on the anxious end of the spectrum they'll go home and study their face and they actually look at their face as if for the first time in many ways and i've had patients notice things that have always been there for sure little blood vessels freckles lines wrinkles asymmetries they will often come back and say i don't think this was like this before you did the treatment now most often that fits with an anxious patient and what i think the most useful thing is first of all what do you do to minimize the anxiety for your patient before they've even left so if you can give them a sense of certainty during the process of delivering the treatment and afterwards they will leave and hopefully look for the positive result

the positive result now if they are on the anxious end of the spectrum they will actually go away with fear in their mind and look for negatives and if you look in the mirror and you look for long enough and you look with fear with your fear glasses on as i sometimes say um you will actually see faults rather than things to appreciate and this is what i think happens with anxious patients the ones i've known over the years you've come back with you know you've done some toxins around my eyes and now i have a freckle it was definitely never there and they often say this key thing which is i think i know my face and i will most often have a photograph and then actually show them so this is the key obviously now firstly um listen to them don't dismiss them if they're anxious about something you need to hear that you need to understand exactly what it is they're concerned about why then you need to get the photographs out to check that you're both on the same page i'd say eight out of ten times it'll be something that you can actually point out was there already and the other 20 of the time it can be really subtle and be like i think the cheek is a bit more you know curved or there's a different shape or it's slightly heavy looking and it doesn't always show in the photograph

yeah in which case you need to first of all make sure that you've heard them properly you can then start to discuss the idea that perception is changing things and um so i always say to patients that you're psychological for all of us so me included it's changed by the emotional tone you're looking at with so if they are scared or anxious they are more likely to spot a fault as we've already said um and this sometimes in some people at least open their minds the to the chance that they're actually imagining this now before you get to that point maybe not imagining but kind of uh it's more that you exaggerate something that may be there already a natural asymmetry which suddenly looks more obvious after the treatment now of course we can't too quickly dismiss this so you need to also get back to what your original treatment plan is have a look at how you injected them have a look at your before pictures and see if you can really make sense of the fact that there could be a problem because often the patient will be talking about something that's nowhere near the place that you're treated and you can you then must reassure them that this is nothing to do the treatment is too far away or use the wrong dose so the

product you use could never cause that and that should at least help things a little bit if they all accept that reassurance and i will say that the less sure you are in your tone the less they will take your word for it and you need to be certain in yourself and convey that to them i think many clinicians often you feel slightly on the back foot when it's something you don't entirely see yourself and they they're saying that it's there and you don't know how to handle it and if that lack of certainty comes across in the way you communicate it you may find that they just simply don't take your advice so when you feel confident state it confidently what you believe the situation is and why you why they shouldn't worry and hopefully that will help give the confidence that they need that they can go away and just actually try and enjoy the result and sort of look for faults in it now of course most of the most important thing of all is make sure you're right about this because sometimes you will have caused an issue and it's our responsibility as clinicians to make sure that we get our patients in a happy and healthy state and so don't dismiss them too soon i see a lot of clinicians very quickly dismissing patients as dysmorphic

and it's not a good idea you need to listen to them carefully see it from their side make sure they understand that you see from their side and only then try and change their opinion if you believe that they are incorrect in their assessment and putting all of that together in most cases you'll come out with a patient who understands more but it does involve a lot of talking so you can't do this quickly you need to talk and listen and explain use mirrors use photographs and all together you should get a good result in most people hope that helps
